title: Lisa The Skeptic

jokes.text: we arrest them and beat them to the full extent of the law
jokes.setup: Chief Wiggum explaining their police sting operation
jokes.punchline: Mixing 'prosecute to the full extent of the law' with literal beating
jokes.explanation: Perfect malapropism that reveals Wiggum's incompetence while being structurally sound - 'beat them' works both ways

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: We're going to prosecute them to the full extent of the law.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: And then we're going to beat them.

jokes.text: So the hook is baited. Nice metaphor, Eddie. Yeah, good work, Eddie.
jokes.setup: Wiggum uses fishing metaphor for their sting
jokes.punchline: His subordinates earnestly praise his basic metaphor usage
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing the police force's low standards for intellectual achievement

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: We're gonna use a sting operation. Think of it like fishing - we cast out our line, and the crooks bite.

dialogue.speaker: Eddie
dialogue.line: That's brilliant, Chief! A fishing metaphor!

dialogue.speaker: Lou
dialogue.line: Yeah, real clever stuff there, Chief.

jokes.text: You're the most paranoid family I've ever been affiliated with
jokes.setup: Family suspects the obvious police sting
jokes.punchline: Homer calls THEM paranoid for being suspicious
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer obliviousness - he's the one falling for an obvious scam but calls others paranoid

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You guys are being paranoid. There's no police sting. That's ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, there's a van outside with 'Free Donuts' written on it and it's full of police officers.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So? That doesn't mean anything. You're all just paranoid!

jokes.text: I call brutality on you
jokes.setup: Snake gets arrested and beaten by police
jokes.punchline: Uses 'call brutality' like calling a foul in sports
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay treating police brutality like a sports penalty - economical and character-appropriate

dialogue.speaker: Snake
dialogue.line: Hey man, that's call brutality!

jokes.text: 235 unpaid parking tickets totaling $175
jokes.setup: Homer's massive number of parking violations
jokes.punchline: The enormous quantity costs less than one modern parking ticket
jokes.explanation: Absurd math joke that gets funnier when you think about it - 235 tickets for less than a dollar each

dialogue.speaker: Lou
dialogue.line: You got 10,000 parking violations here. That'll be $200.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Two hundred dollars? That's a bargain!

jokes.text: I didn't like it. The mast had termites. Why would a motorboat have a mast? Because! The thing, it was- Shut up!
jokes.setup: Lisa asks why Homer didn't get the motorboat
jokes.punchline: Homer's increasingly desperate lies collapse under basic logic
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - caught in a lie, he doubles down with worse lies until giving up completely

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, why didn't you get the motorboat?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I was going to, but then I realized the water was too wet.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Water is always wet, Dad.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Exactly! So I couldn't take that risk.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sure it does! Besides, the boat was a boat, and boats are for people who like boats, and I don't like boats!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You've wanted a boat your entire life.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That was the old Homer. New Homer is afraid of boats. And water. And wet things.

jokes.text: They say the air conditioner will be more powerful than a million hydrogen bombs
jokes.setup: Homer describing the new mega-mall
jokes.punchline: Comparing AC power to nuclear weapons in casual tone
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd hyperbole delivered matter-of-factly - captures American mall excess perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, this new mega-mall is incredible. They've got air conditioning so powerful, it's like they harnessed the power of a thousand nuclear reactors.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm not saying it's a weapon, but if it were, America would finally have something to be proud of.

jokes.text: Fossils, schmossils. Bones, schmones.
jokes.setup: Lisa worried about paving over fossil site
jokes.punchline: Homer dismisses everything with Yiddish-style repetition
jokes.explanation: Standard 'schmuck' repetition joke - functional but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But Dad, they're going to pave over the fossil site!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Fossil, shmossil. It's just old rocks, schmold rocks. Who cares, schmoo cares?

jokes.text: My attorney, Lionel Hutz, calls your attention to Municipal Code 147-C
jokes.setup: Lisa invoking legal authority
jokes.punchline: She's represented by the town's notoriously incompetent lawyer
jokes.explanation: Mild callback joke - the humor is in Hutz's reputation preceding him

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'm afraid you'll have to take this up with my lawyer.

dialogue.speaker: Lionel Hutz
dialogue.line: Hello, I'm Troy McClure. Wait, no... Lionel Hutz, attorney at law. I can help you with that.

jokes.text: Mr. Hutz, we won. We?
jokes.setup: Lisa tells Hutz they won the case
jokes.punchline: Hutz immediately distances himself from shared victory
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hutz moment - takes credit for nothing, avoids responsibility for everything, captured in one word

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: We won the case!

dialogue.speaker: Lionel Hutz
dialogue.line: We? I think you mean you won the case. I was merely a silent observer.

jokes.text: All honor students will be rewarded with a trip to an archeological dig! Conversely, all detention students will be punished with a trip to an archeological dig.
jokes.setup: Announcement about field trip as reward
jokes.punchline: The exact same trip is simultaneously punishment
jokes.explanation: Brilliant structural joke about school's arbitrary punishment/reward system - perfectly delivered parallel construction

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: As a reward for perfect attendance, we're taking a field trip to the Springfield Nuclear Power Plant.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: As punishment for the trouble you've caused, detention will also be at the Springfield Nuclear Power Plant.

jokes.text: Prinskipper Skippal Prinnipple Skipper
jokes.setup: Ralph trying to call Principal Skinner
jokes.punchline: Completely mangles his name in classic Ralph fashion
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph moment - his unique way of mangling words in a lovably confused way

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Principal Skinner? More like Principal Skinflint!

jokes.text: That's your trowel blade, Ralph. It fell off the handle. And I found it!
jokes.setup: Ralph excitedly announces his archaeological discovery
jokes.punchline: He 'discovered' his own broken tool and remains proud
jokes.explanation: Classic Ralph obliviousness - even when corrected, he finds pride in his 'discovery'

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: I found a dinosaur!

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Ralph, that's just a chicken bone.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: My precious Venus de Milo!

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: That's your broken ruler from last week.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: It's the rarest dinosaur of all!

jokes.text: A bottle cap, a shoe, a bar of gold- anything!
jokes.setup: Lisa desperately hoping to find something
jokes.punchline: Escalates from trash to treasure in her desperation
jokes.explanation: Good escalation joke showing Lisa's growing desperation through increasingly valuable items

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mom? Dad? Bart? Anyone? Please, I need help!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'll check the trash can. Maybe there's something useful in here.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: A half-eaten sandwich? Okay, that's not great, but I could work with—

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Wait, is that a piece of eight? An actual Spanish doubloon?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: This changes everything! I'm rich! I'M RICH!

jokes.text: from the looks of it, I'd say this fellow died from causes unknown
jokes.setup: Dr. Hibbert examining mysterious skeletal remains
jokes.punchline: States the obvious about a skeleton's cause of death
jokes.explanation: Mild medical humor - doctor stating obvious about skeleton's death

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, the cause of death appears to be... bones.

jokes.text: Speaking from a strictly medical point of view, that ain't right
jokes.setup: Examining the wing-like bone structure
jokes.punchline: Medical professional's technical diagnosis is 'that ain't right'
jokes.explanation: Perfect contrast between formal medical authority and colloquial assessment - very quotable

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, that ain't right.

jokes.text: Well, if you're so sure what it ain't, how about tellin' us what it am?
jokes.setup: Lisa saying it's not an angel
jokes.punchline: Cletus challenges her with hilariously bad grammar ('what it am')
jokes.explanation: Perfect Cletus line - makes a logical point while completely butchering grammar in character

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: It's not an angel.

dialogue.speaker: Cletus
dialogue.line: Well, what it am then?

jokes.text: a Neanderthal who got bitten by some angry fish
jokes.setup: Lisa pressed to explain the skeleton
jokes.punchline: Comes up with increasingly ridiculous scientific explanation under pressure
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment - smart Lisa forced into absurd explanation, showing even she can be flustered

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Well, you see, the skeletal structure is a calcium-based framework that provides structural support for the body's soft tissues and organs.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: It's also a repository for bone marrow, which produces red blood cells essential for oxygen transport throughout the circulatory system.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: And obviously, the skeleton serves as a protective cage for vital organs like the heart, lungs, and brain.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Plus, bones are actually living tissue that constantly remodel themselves through osteoblast and osteoclast activity, maintaining homeostatic balance.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: And if you really want to get technical, skeletons are basically nature's way of keeping us from being just a puddle of organs on the floor.

jokes.text: It sounds like you're strainin' to do some explainin'.
jokes.setup: Lisa struggles to provide scientific explanation
jokes.punchline: Moe delivers folksy rhyming dismissal
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay that fits Moe's character. The rhyme scheme adds folksy charm to his anti-intellectual stance.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Science? Bah! That's just fancy talk for stuff we don't understand. Now let me tell ya something, kid - if it don't make sense to ol' Moe, then it ain't worth a plugged nickel. Science, schience - it's all hooey and folderol!

jokes.text: everyone's heard of angels, but who's ever heard of a 'Neanderthal'?
jokes.setup: Lisa's scientific explanation vs. angel theory
jokes.punchline: Moe thinks Neanderthals are more obscure than angels
jokes.explanation: Good satire of anti-intellectualism - scientific fact treated as more far-fetched than religious miracle

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Neanderthals were an early human species that went extinct about 40,000 years ago due to evolutionary pressure.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well I've never heard of 'em. Now angels, everybody knows about angels.

jokes.text: Oh, fiddle-faddle. Everyone knows our mutants have flippers.
jokes.setup: Suggestion the skeleton could be a nuclear plant mutant
jokes.punchline: Burns casually admits to creating flippered mutants
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor - Burns casually admits to environmental crimes while dismissing the evidence on technicality

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Oh yes, well, I may have created a few... mutants in my time. Nothing to be ashamed of, really. Why, some of my best employees have had extra limbs.

jokes.text: Smithers, use the amnesia ray. You mean, the revolver, sir? Precisely.
jokes.setup: Burns wants to erase memories of his admission
jokes.punchline: The 'amnesia ray' is just a gun for killing witnesses
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark joke with perfect setup/payoff - euphemistic 'amnesia ray' revealed as murder weapon, perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, I need you to erase all memories of my recent admission. Fetch the amnesia ray at once.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Very good, sir. I'll retrieve it from the vault.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Excellent. Soon no one will remember a thing.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, I should mention... the amnesia ray is just a gun for killing witnesses.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Even better!

jokes.text: I'll need to refer to the case of Finders versus Keepers
jokes.setup: Legal dispute over skeleton ownership
jokes.punchline: Hutz cites playground rules as legal precedent
jokes.explanation: Classic Hutz incompetence - treating children's saying as actual legal precedent with complete seriousness

dialogue.speaker: Lionel Hutz
dialogue.line: Your Honor, I move that we settle this matter according to the ancient and binding rules of the playground. Finders keepers, losers weepers.

jokes.text: So long, suckers!
jokes.setup: Discussion about sharing the skeleton
jokes.punchline: Homer steals it while others talk about cooperation
jokes.explanation: Standard Homer greed moment - perfectly timed interruption of civic cooperation

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: While you guys are talking about sharing, I'm taking this bad boy home!

jokes.text: That's what they said about this Billy Beer, smarty-pants.
jokes.setup: Lisa saying the fossil can't get more valuable
jokes.punchline: Homer compares it to notoriously worthless Billy Carter beer
jokes.explanation: Good period reference - Billy Beer was famously unsuccessful, making Homer's investment strategy absurd

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: It can't get any more valuable than this.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sure it can. It's like Billy Carter beer — the longer you keep it, the more it's worth.

jokes.text: Get your own angel, you moocher!
jokes.setup: Ned asking to share a prayer with the angel
jokes.punchline: Homer treats spiritual experience like personal property
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer selfishness - commodifying religious experience, perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: May I share a prayer with the angel?

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: No way, Flanders. I saw her first. She's mine.

jokes.text: Hey, I'm trying to eat here! Beat it, peg leg!
jokes.setup: Agnes asking for good luck before foot surgery
jokes.punchline: Homer cruelly mocks her medical condition
jokes.explanation: Harsh character moment showing Homer's selfishness overriding any human compassion

dialogue.speaker: Agnes
dialogue.line: Homer, wish me luck. I'm going in for foot surgery.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Good luck! You're gonna need it with those feet. They look like two potatoes that got into a fight!

jokes.text: If you look carefully, you'll notice I never once used the word 'angel'
jokes.setup: Lisa pointing out he's claiming it's an angel
jokes.punchline: Homer's technicality while clearly implying it's an angel
jokes.explanation: Good example of Homer's child-like logic - technically correct but completely missing the point

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, you're claiming it's an angel.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I said it was *angelic*. There's a difference.

jokes.text: I didn't become a scientist for financial gain. Whatever little money you have will be just fine.
jokes.setup: Professor claiming noble motivations
jokes.punchline: Immediately asks for money anyway
jokes.explanation: Classic hypocrisy joke - claims pure motives then immediately contradicts himself

dialogue.speaker: Professor Farnsworth
dialogue.line: I'm not in this for the money, I'm in it for the science!

dialogue.speaker: Professor Farnsworth
dialogue.line: Now, who wants to chip in fifty bucks for lab supplies?

jokes.text: Facts are meaningless. You can use facts to prove anything that's even remotely true
jokes.setup: Lisa presenting scientific facts
jokes.punchline: Homer's backwards logic about the nature of facts
jokes.explanation: Brilliant anti-intellectual satire - Homer's logic is simultaneously completely wrong and oddly insightful about confirmation bias

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Facts are objective truths established through the scientific method.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's where you're wrong, Lisa. Facts are just theories that haven't been disproven yet. And if I don't believe in them, they don't exist.

jokes.text: Can I use your bathroom?
jokes.setup: Professor running up excitedly with test results
jokes.punchline: He was just running to find a bathroom, not deliver news
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - builds anticipation for scientific revelation, deflates it with mundane need

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: I have the test results! I have the test results!

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: Wait, no, I just really need to use the bathroom.

jokes.text: No one gets into heaven without a glow stick
jokes.setup: Homer selling angel merchandise
jokes.punchline: Creates fake religious requirement for his cheap product
jokes.explanation: Great satire of commercialized religion - Homer invents theological doctrine to sell novelties

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, the Bible clearly states you need to wear a halo at all times. It's right there in... Deuteronomy... uh... 5:12. 'Thou shalt wear Homer's Halo Caps, only $19.95.'

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: That doesn't sound right.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Look, do you want to go to Heaven or not?

jokes.text: Ach! Those morons make me so angry. Maybe so. But I'd appreciate it if you didn't call them morons. But they are morons.
jokes.setup: Willie calling the crowd morons, Marge objecting
jokes.punchline: Willie's blunt insistence on calling them what he sees them as
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Willie's stubborn honesty vs. Marge's politeness

dialogue.speaker: Groundskeeper Willie
dialogue.line: Ye are all morons!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Now, that's not a very nice thing to say.

dialogue.speaker: Groundskeeper Willie
dialogue.line: Well, if the shoe fits! Ye wandered into me groundskeeper's shed, ye took me best weed whacker, and ye nearly blew up me prize-winnin' petunias! So aye, ye are morons, and I'll nae apologize fer tellin' the truth!

jokes.text: Everyone knows leprechauns are extinct
jokes.setup: Lisa listing mythical creatures as parallel to angels
jokes.punchline: Barney treats leprechauns as real but extinct rather than mythical
jokes.explanation: Brilliant drunk logic - misses Lisa's point about mythology, treats fantasy creatures as extinct species

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: So leprechauns are like the angels of Irish mythology.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Leprechauns aren't mythology, they're real. They're just extinct.

jokes.text: Science is like a blabbermouth who ruins a movie by telling you how it ends
jokes.setup: Argument about science vs. faith
jokes.punchline: Barney's surprisingly poetic anti-science metaphor
jokes.explanation: Unexpectedly insightful metaphor from unlikely source - captures anti-intellectual sentiment perfectly while being genuinely clever

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Science? Science is just faith in a lab coat. You're asking me to believe in invisible particles and dead dinosaurs. At least the Bible has pictures!

jokes.text: Oh! I'm paralyzed. I just hope medical science can cure me
jokes.setup: Professor gets hurt in anti-science riot
jokes.punchline: Immediately relies on the science he was just defending
jokes.explanation: Good ironic moment - shows how quickly people turn to science when they need it

dialogue.speaker: Professor Farnsworth
dialogue.line: Oh no! The anti-science mob is attacking me!

dialogue.speaker: Professor Farnsworth
dialogue.line: Wait, I need to calculate the trajectory of these rocks using trigonometry to dodge them!

jokes.text: Why? Why was I programmed to feel pain?
jokes.setup: Robot getting beaten in the riot
jokes.punchline: Questions the cruel logic of its programming
jokes.explanation: Existential robot humor - questioning the pointless cruelty of giving machines the capacity to suffer

dialogue.speaker: Robot
dialogue.line: Why must I be programmed to feel pain? What cruel logic dictates that I suffer?

jokes.text: Hey, she's gonna smash the angel! Somebody stop her!
jokes.setup: Lisa goes to the garage with a crowbar
jokes.punchline: Crowd assumes she's destroying evidence rather than investigating
jokes.explanation: Standard mob mentality moment - jumping to conclusions about Lisa's intentions

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I'm going to investigate what happened to the car with this crowbar.

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: She's destroying evidence! Get her!

jokes.text: What the hell are we gonna do with 10,000 angel ashtrays? I could take up smoking. You damn well better.
jokes.setup: Homer has too much angel merchandise
jokes.punchline: Marge offers to start smoking, Homer encourages the unhealthy habit
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about Homer's desperation - willing to encourage wife's smoking habit for profit

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you have to get rid of all this angel stuff. It's completely out of control.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But Marge, angels are real and they love me!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, if it'll make you happy, I'll start smoking again.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's the spirit! Light one up right now!

jokes.text: Bart, strip down to your skeleton
jokes.setup: Homer needs replacement skeleton
jokes.punchline: Casually asks son to die for the scam
jokes.explanation: Dark Homer moment - treating his son's death as casual solution to business problem

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey Bart, you know what would really help me out? If you died.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, what?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Just for insurance purposes. It'll only be temporary.

jokes.text: Give her a nice cell- something in C Block
jokes.setup: Wiggum arresting Lisa
jokes.punchline: Treats child's cell assignment like hotel accommodations
jokes.explanation: Mild Wiggum incompetence - treating jail like hospitality service

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Now Lisa, you're going to be in cell block D. We've got a nice cot, a toilet that only occasionally backs up, and a small window where you can see the exercise yard... or as I like to call it, the 'yard of regret.'

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, this is a jail cell.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: I know, I know. Tell you what, I'll have them move you to a cell with a view. Eddie! Move her to the east wing!

jokes.text: Religion must stay 500 yards from Science at all times
jokes.setup: Judge resolving science vs. religion case
jokes.punchline: Issues restraining order between abstract concepts
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist legal humor - treating philosophical concepts as people who can be legally separated

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: I hereby issue a restraining order between science and religion. They are to stay at least 500 feet apart at all times.

jokes.text: I'm scared, Daddy. Too scared to even wet my pants. It's okay, Son. Just relax, and it'll come.
jokes.setup: Bart afraid of the apocalypse
jokes.punchline: Homer encourages pants-wetting rather than addressing fears
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer parenting - completely misses the emotional need, focuses on literal bodily function

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, I'm afraid of the apocalypse.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Son, don't worry about the apocalypse. Just wet your pants like the rest of us.

jokes.text: This doomsday warning has me just a smidge twitterpated
jokes.setup: Ned expressing fear about apocalypse
jokes.punchline: Uses cutesy euphemism for being terrified
jokes.explanation: Good Ned characterization - his overly polite vocabulary even when facing death

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, I'm not scared... I'm just a teensy-weensy bit worried about the end times!

jokes.text: Well, you might say we're going to the best steakhouse in the whole universe. So we're not going to Black Angus.
jokes.setup: Marge euphemistically describing heaven as best steakhouse
jokes.punchline: Bart correctly deduces this excludes Black Angus
jokes.explanation: Good setup/payoff - Bart's logic that the universe's best steakhouse wouldn't be Black Angus

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Heaven is like the best steakhouse in the world.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: So Black Angus is excluded?

jokes.text: We did it. We beat cancer
jokes.setup: Countdown to apocalypse reaches zero
jokes.punchline: Barney celebrates surviving with irrelevant medical victory
jokes.explanation: Random Barney moment - connects apocalypse survival to unrelated medical achievement

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! We survived the apocalypse! I'm gonna live forever!

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: And my liver function tests came back normal!

jokes.text: The end of high prices!
jokes.setup: Apocalyptic proclamation 'Prepare for the end!'
jokes.punchline: Revealed as mall advertising slogan
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection - perfect setup for commercial subversion of apocalyptic expectations

dialogue.speaker: Mall Angel
dialogue.line: Prepare for the end!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh my God, the end is nigh!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: We're doomed!

dialogue.speaker: Mall Angel
dialogue.line: ...of season clearance at The Anvil Store!

jokes.text: Out of my way, shorty!
jokes.setup: Crowd angry about being deceived
jokes.punchline: Instantly forgets outrage when sees shopping deals
jokes.explanation: Standard consumer satire - moral outrage forgotten when shopping opportunities arise

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Yeah, we've been deceived! We're outraged!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, did someone say shopping deals?

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Where?! We forgot what we were mad about!

jokes.text: I'm gonna be honest with you, Lisa. I never did the tests
jokes.setup: Lisa asking why tests didn't reveal fake skeleton
jokes.punchline: Professor admits to scientific negligence
jokes.explanation: Reveals the incompetence behind earlier 'inconclusive' results - scientist didn't do science

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But Professor, how did the tests not reveal that the skeleton was fake?

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: Well, you see, I may have... uh... skipped the standard verification procedures. And possibly filed false reports. And I might have been napping during the entire examination.

jokes.text: Well, it was just so loud... and- [Laughs] thanks for squeezing back.
jokes.setup: Homer points out Lisa was scared when angel spoke
jokes.punchline: Lisa admits fear while maintaining embarrassment about it
jokes.explanation: Sweet character moment showing Lisa's vulnerability beneath her intellectual confidence. Good emotional beat.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You were scared when the angel spoke.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I was not scared. I was just... startled by the sudden appearance of a celestial being.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You jumped three feet in the air.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That doesn't mean I was scared. It was merely an involuntary physiological response to an unexpected stimulus.

tmdb_overview: When a skeleton is unearthed at the site of a future shopping mall, all Springfield is convinced it is the skeleton of an angel – except Lisa.
insight: Lisa's skepticism carries 50 jokes through absurdist logic and character irony at peak Nineties form.
